mappings.rationaleAI: Traces logic from Aristotle's syllogistic reasoning (A.1.b) through Frege's reconception as rigorous formal discipline (A.3.c) to Gödel's incompleteness theorems (B.1.b) and Russell's logicist program (A.4.a).

Gödel's incompleteness theorems address the conflict between Hilbert's Formalism and Russell's Logicism (A.4.a), constitute metalogical results about axiomatized systems (A.4.c), and demonstrate limitations of formal logical systems (B.1.b).

Russell's attempt to reduce mathematics to logic exemplifies Logicism (A.4.a), Russell's Paradox exposed problems in Frege's system (A.3.c), and his theory of descriptions shaped logic as a discipline (B.1.a).

The analytic tradition founded by Russell, Frege, and Wittgenstein placed formal logic at philosophy's center, shaping conceptions of logic's scope (B.1.a) and reflecting different approaches to philosophical method (A.4).

Ordinary language philosophy challenged formal logic's dominance, presenting alternative conception of logic's scope (B.1.a), connecting to problems in meaning and truth (B.2.a) and alternatives to formal logics (B.2.c).
Correspondence, coherence, pragmatist, and semantic theories of truth directly address problems in meaning and truth central to philosophy of logic (B.2.a).
Medieval universities adopted logic as core trivium discipline, providing institutional setting for disputes between old and new logic after translation of Aristotle's Organon (A.2.a).
The twelfth-century renaissance channeled Arabic translations of Aristotle back into Latin scholarship, enabling recovery of the Organon that sparked old/new logic disputes (A.2.a).

The Vienna Circle's work was foundational to 20th-century logic, engaging the conflict between Logicism, Intuitionism, and Formalism (A.4.a) and developments in propositional and predicate logic (A.4.b).
